review_summary: Textual grounding is sufficient for concept-focused questions, and no separate video clip is needed.

question_bank.question: In distributed data parallel training, what does each GPU do before synchronization?
choices: It runs the same model on a different slice of data and computes gradients for that slice.
It owns a different layer of the model and never stores the full model.
It computes only optimizer statistics while another GPU computes gradients.
It receives identical data so all activations and gradients are identical before communication.
question_bank.explanation: DDP replicates the model and splits the data. Each worker sees different examples, so its local gradients differ until communication combines them into a shared update.

question_bank.question: What does an all-reduce operation provide in DDP?
choices: It combines gradients across workers and gives the combined result back to every worker.
It sends each layer's parameters to only one worker for storage.
It deletes optimizer state after every update to save memory.
It chooses the worker with the smallest gradient and copies its result to the others.
question_bank.explanation: All-reduce is the synchronization step that makes every replica update consistently by sharing the aggregated gradient information across workers.

question_bank.question: Why does plain DDP have poor memory scaling for large models?
choices: Each GPU still stores the full model, full gradients, FP32 master weights, and optimizer state.
Only one GPU stores gradients, so other GPUs must wait for it to finish.
The model must be copied from CPU memory at every layer.
Every parameter is stored as text rather than binary floating point.
question_bank.explanation: DDP improves data throughput but not model-state memory much, because each replica keeps nearly all training state locally.

question_bank.question: What does ZeRO stage 1 shard across GPUs?
choices: The optimizer state, while each GPU still keeps the full FP16 model parameters.
The input tokens, while all optimizer state is duplicated.
Only the activations from the first transformer layer.
The attention heads, while gradients are never synchronized.
question_bank.explanation: ZeRO stage 1 reduces memory by partitioning optimizer-related state. Workers update the parameter shards they are responsible for and then gather updated parameters to stay synchronized.
